channel.title: NYT > World > Americas
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Spirit Airlines Cancels All Flights, Stranding Passengers
item.description: The budget carrier abruptly canceled flights early on Saturday, leaving passengers to rush to make other plans. “Even if they go back into business, never again,” one traveler said.
item.dc:creator: Rylee Kirk
item.media:description: Some passengers were in the middle of their trips when they learned that their Spirit Airlines flights had been canceled.

item.title: Planning a World Cup Watch Party at a Bar? The ‘FIFA Police’ Are Lurking
item.description: Some businesses advertising watch parties in Canada’s two host cities are wary of running afoul of FIFA’s copyright, which protects advertising “World Cup” events.
item.dc:creator: Vjosa Isai
item.media:description: Fans watching a FIFA World Cup match between Brazil and Cameroon at a bar in Toronto in 2014.

item.title: Most Canadians Support MAID. But the Issue of Access for People With Mental Illness Is Fraught.
item.description: As Canada weighs the future of medically assisted death, one woman fears the law will change too late for her.
item.dc:creator: Stephanie Nolen
item.media:credit: Chloe Ellingson for The New York Times
item.media:description: Claire Brosseau in her Toronto home with her dog, Olive, in 2025.

item.title: After Trump’s Ousting of Maduro, Little Has Changed for Most Venezuelans
item.description: U.S. officials say they will “unleash prosperity” by commandeering the oil industry. Many people in Caracas say it will take far more than that.
item.dc:creator: Max Bearak and Adriana Loureiro Fernandez
item.media:credit: Adriana Loureiro Fernandez for The New York Times
item.media:description: People ride on a crowded bus during rush hour in Caricuao, Venezuela. A one-way bus ticket costs around $0.15 — half of the nation’s hourly minimum wage.

item.title: Mexican Governor Accused by U.S. of Aiding Drug Cartel Steps Down
item.description: U.S. prosecutors accused Rubén Rocha Moya of protecting the powerful Sinaloa Cartel in exchange for bribes and political support, setting off a national scandal.
item.dc:creator: Jack Nicas
item.media:description: Rubén Rocha Moya, the governor of the Mexican state of Sinaloa, said he had decided to take “a temporary leave of absence.”

item.title: Why U.S. Officials Are in Venezuela Right Now
item.description: U.S. officials visited Venezuela after the first direct flight between the two countries since 2019. Our White House correspondent Tyler Pager describes how the main message of the trip was, in the words of a White House adviser “drill, baby, drill.”
item.dc:creator: Tyler Pager, Melanie Bencosme and Todd Heisler

item.title: On May Day Cuba’s Communist Leaders Deliver a Defiant Tone to the U.S.
item.description: In front of the American Embassy in Havana, the Cuban government held its annual International Worker’s Day celebration as the United States sought to end the communist ruling system.
item.dc:creator: Frances Robles
item.media:credit: Lisette Poole for The New York Times
item.media:description: May Day celebrations took place in Havana early Friday morning.

item.title: U.S. Indictment of Sinaloa’s Governor Is a Reckoning for Residents of the Mexican State
item.description: The U.S. indictment of a Mexican governor confirms what many residents say they had long suspected: The line between organized crime and the upper echelons of government has blurred.
item.dc:creator: Paulina Villegas
item.media:credit: Adriana Zehbrauskas for The New York Times
item.media:description: Members of the Mexican National Guard in Culiacán, Sinaloa, in February.

item.title: In ‘Our Land,’ Lucrecia Martel Turns Her Camera on a Killing
item.description: Technology, history and human lives intersect in Lucrecia Martel’s examination of the death of an Indigenous leader in Argentina.
item.dc:creator: Alissa Wilkinson

item.title: After Seven Years, First U.S. Direct Flight Lands in Caracas
item.description: President Trump banned commercial flights to Venezuela during his first term, but the flights are the latest step in re-establishing ties between the two countries.
item.dc:creator: Tyler Pager
item.media:description: The first direct flight between the United States and Venezuela landed in Caracas on Thursday afternoon.

item.title: Sheinbaum’s Dilemma in Mexico: Defy the U.S. or Arrest an Ally
item.description: The Trump administration wants President Claudia Sheinbaum to arrest a Mexican governor. She is faced with few good options in response.
item.dc:creator: Jack Nicas and Emiliano Rodríguez Mega
item.media:description: President Claudia Sheinbaum of Mexico could arrest one of her party allies, the governor of Sinaloa state, and extradite him to the United States, or defy the U.S. request and protect him.

item.title: U.S. Indictment Accuses Mexican Governor of Conspiring With Sinaloa Cartel
item.description: Prosecutors accused Rubén Rocha Moya, the governor of Sinaloa state, and other Mexican officials of a yearslong conspiracy to protect the powerful cartel.
item.dc:creator: Jack Nicas, Maria Abi-Habib and Emiliano Rodríguez Mega
item.media:description: Rubén Rocha Moya, the governor of Sinaloa state, is the highest-ranking member of Mexico’s dominant political party, Morena, to be indicted by the United States.

item.title: Epstein Obtained Objects From Islam’s Holiest Site for His Island ‘Mosque’
item.description: Jeffrey Epstein’s messages cast light on an unusual building on his private island and show how his connections helped him secure tapestries from Mecca for it.
item.dc:creator: Ephrat Livni

item.title: Prime Minister Mark Carney Says Canada’s Economy Is Expected to Grow and Deficit to Fall
item.description: Prime Minister Mark Carney’s budget update keeps to his path of diversifying away from the United States.
item.dc:creator: Ian Austen
item.media:credit: Sean Kilpatrick/The Canadian Press, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Prime Minister Mark Carney of Canada arriving at Parliament Hill in Ottawa on Tuesday.

item.title: Mexico Says 4 Foreigners Were at Cartel Raid Where 2 C.I.A. Officers Died
item.description: Mexican officials revealed that four foreigners — not two — were present during a counterdrug operation in northern Mexico where a crash killed two men later identified as C.I.A. officers.
item.dc:creator: Paulina Villegas
item.media:description: A suspected methamphetamine lab in a remote part of the state of Chihuahua, Mexico.

item.title: They Lost Their Homes in Cuba to the Communist Government. Will They Ever Get Them Back?
item.description: With Cuba in dire economic crisis, people whose properties were seized by its government decades ago say it’s time to resolve thorny compensation claims.
item.dc:creator: Frances Robles
item.media:credit: Scott McIntyre for The New York Times
item.media:description: Teo A. Babún, Jr., in Miami this month. Consultants hired by his family estimated the value of its lost holdings in Cuba to be in the hundreds of millions of dollars by 2018.

item.title: Ron Paul Saw The U.S. Mint’s Gold Problems Coming
item.description: And he’s basically fine with it.
item.dc:creator: Justin Scheck
item.category: Human Rights and Human Rights Violations
item.media:credit: CQ Roll Call, via Getty Images
item.media:description: Ron Paul, then a Texas congressman for the Republican Party, in 1983.

item.title: Canada’s New Route to Citizenship Has Thousands of Americans Lining Up to Apply
item.description: Canada has opened a route to citizenship for people who can prove they have a Canada-born ancestor. Millions could qualify, and Americans are already lining up to apply.
item.dc:creator: Vjosa Isai and Matina Stevis-Gridneff
item.media:description: A citizenship ceremony for new Canadians last year in Montreal.

item.title: Carney to Launch Sovereign Wealth Fund to Distance Canada’s Economy From U.S.
item.description: The sovereign wealth fund announced by Prime Minister Mark Carney will be far smaller than ones in other oil producers like Norway and the Middle East.
item.dc:creator: Ian Austen
item.media:description: Prime Minister Mark Carney of Canada announcing a sovereign wealth fund on Monday in Ottawa.

item.title: Canada Says Its Gold Is Traceable and Clean. So We Traced It.
item.description: We ended up in a Colombian mine controlled by a drug cartel.
item.dc:creator: Justin Scheck
item.category: Human Rights and Human Rights Violations
Organization for Economic Cooperation and Development
item.media:credit: Federico Rios for The New York Times
item.media:description: This illegal mine near Caucasia, Colombia, sells gold into the Royal Canadian Mint’s supply chain.

item.title: U.S. Military Strikes Another Boat in Eastern Pacific, Killing 3
item.description: The United States has carried out dozens of boat strikes since September against people the Trump administration accuses of smuggling drugs.
item.dc:creator: Jin Yu Young
item.category: US Strikes on Alleged Drug Traffickers (2025- )
item.media:description: An image from a video provided by the U.S. military shows what it said was a strike on a boat in the eastern Pacific Ocean on Sunday.

item.title: What Happened After The New York Times Found a Cartel Mine on a Colombian Military Base
item.description: Officers denied that an illegal, large-scale gold operation was underway within earshot of their posts. But we had seen it with our own eyes.
item.dc:creator: Federico Rios

item.title: How Drug Cartel Gold Ends Up at the U.S. Mint
item.description: The U.S. Mint is legally required to sell only legal, domestic gold. Instead, it is the last link in a chain that launders foreign gold for an insatiable market. Our reporter Justin Scheck traced one such supply chain: from an illegal mine in Colombia all the way to the Mint’s facilities in West Point, N.Y.
item.dc:creator: Justin Scheck, Rebecca Suner, James Surdam, Lauren Pruitt and Federico Rios

item.title: U.S. Mint Buys Drug Cartel Gold and Sells It as ‘American’
item.description: As prices for the precious metal soar, the industry’s guardrails have broken down.
item.dc:creator: Justin Scheck, Simón Posada and Federico Rios
item.media:description:
This gold comes from a Colombian drug cartel mine. It should never end up at the U.S. Mint.
But it does.
